{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/30039","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/30039/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/30039/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/30039/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/30039","id":380866556,"node_id":"MDU6SXNzdWUzODA4NjY1NTY=","number":30039,"title":"Julia inconsistent with stdin scripts","user":{"login":"mortenpi","id":147757,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE0Nzc1Nw==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/147757?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mortenpi","html_url":"https://github.com/mortenpi","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mortenpi/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mortenpi/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mortenpi/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mortenpi/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mortenpi/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mortenpi/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mortenpi/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mortenpi/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mortenpi/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":4,"created_at":"2018-11-14T20:09:31Z","updated_at":"2024-11-05T10:09:58Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","sub_issues_summary":{"total":0,"completed":0,"percent_completed":0},"active_lock_reason":null,"body":"The behaviour when passing scripts via stdin in somewhat inconsistent and depends on how you do it exactly. Specifically:

When attaching a file or a heredoc to stdin, Julia does not terminate when it runs into an uncaught error:

```
$ echo 'println("A"); error("B"); println("C")' > test.jl; julia < test.jl; echo "-> $?"
A
ERROR: B
Stacktrace:
 [1] error(::String) at ./error.jl:33
 [2] top-level scope at none:0
C
-> 0
```

```
$ julia <<EOF
println("A"); error("B"); println("C")
EOF  
A
ERROR: B
Stacktrace:
 [1] error(::String) at ./error.jl:33
 [2] top-level scope at none:0
C
$ echo "-> $?"
-> 0
```

Whereas if you pipe, it does (but does not return an error code):

```
$ echo 'println("A"); error("B"); println("C")' | julia; echo "-> $?"
A
ERROR: B
Stacktrace:
 [1] error(::String) at ./error.jl:33
 [2] top-level scope at none:0
-> 0
```

For completeness, this is what happens if you send the same thing in as a script:

```
$ echo 'println("A"); error("B"); println("C") ' > test.jl; julia test.jl; echo "-> $?"
A
ERROR: LoadError: B
Stacktrace:
 [1] error(::String) at ./error.jl:33
 [2] top-level scope at none:0
 [3] include at ./boot.jl:317 [inlined]
 [4] include_relative(::Module, ::String) at ./loading.jl:1044
 [5] include(::Module, ::String) at ./sysimg.jl:29
 [6] exec_options(::Base.JLOptions) at ./client.jl:231
 [7] _start() at ./client.jl:425
in expression starting at /home/mortenpi/Julia/test/test.jl:1
-> 1
```

and with the `-e/E` options:

```
$ julia -e'println("A"); error("B"); println("C")'; echo "-> $?"
A
ERROR: B
Stacktrace:
 [1] error(::String) at ./error.jl:33
 [2] top-level scope at none:0
-> 1
```

I would expect the behaviour to be consistent with how scripts are handled, i.e.:

* Always terminate when an error is thrown, but not caught.
* If there is an error, return an error code.

For comparison, e.g. Python behaves consistently:

```
$ echo 'print("A"); raise(Exception("B")); print("C")' | python3; echo "-> $?"
A
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
Exception: B
-> 1
$ echo 'print("A"); raise(Exception("B")); print("C")' > test.py; python3 < test.py; echo "-> $?"
A
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
Exception: B
-> 1
```

My particular use case is that I would like to have the ability to pass heredocs to Julia in bash scripts, but have them behave like scripts (most importantly, that I could check the error code).

_Environment: Julia 1.0.2 generic binaries on Linux._","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/30039/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/30039/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2456758286","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/30039#issuecomment-2456758286","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/30039","id":2456758286,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM6SbygO","user":{"login":"jkrumbiegel","id":22495855,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIyNDk1ODU1","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/22495855?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jkrumbiegel","html_url":"https://github.com/jkrumbiegel","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jkrumbiegel/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jkrumbiegel/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jkrumbiegel/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jkrumbiegel/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jkrumbiegel/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jkrumbiegel/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jkrumbiegel/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jkrumbiegel/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jkrumbiegel/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-11-05T10:09:56Z","updated_at":"2024-11-05T10:09:56Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"> I am running into this when using Docker HEREDOC with julia

Found this issue while trying to run julia commands in a Docker build script. Just leaving a solution I found here in which julia does not take the HEREDOC input as stdin but as a temporary file, therefore it works as expected for me, it doesn't execute and print line by line, and exit codes are propagated normally.

````
# syntax = docker/dockerfile:1.4

FROM julia:1.10

ENV NTHREADS=3

RUN <<EOF
#!/usr/bin/env -S julia --threads=${NTHREADS}
    if Threads.nthreads() == 3
        @warn("I don't like the number 3")
        exit(120)
    end
EOF
````

This gives the following output with `docker build  --progress=plain`:

```

 Please refer to their respective documentations (especially because they have different behaviors than OpenBLAS with respect to multithreading).
 
+## Execution latency, loading and precompiling time
+
+### Reducing time to first plot etc.
+
+The first time a julia function is called it will be compiled. The [`@time`](@ref) macro family illustrates this.
+
+```
+julia> foo() = rand(2,2) * rand(2,2)
+foo (generic function with 1 method)
+
+julia> @time @eval foo();
+  0.252395 seconds (1.12 M allocations: 56.178 MiB, 2.93% gc time, 98.12% compilation time)
+
+julia> @time @eval foo();
+  0.000156 seconds (63 allocations: 2.453 KiB)
+```
+
+Note that `@time @eval ...` form is explained in the [`@time`](@ref) documentation.","path":"doc/src/manual/performance-tips.md","commit_id":"0c0fdf0489106aa64d5d9eabb5fc358f17310378","original_commit_id":"0c0fdf0489106aa64d5d9eabb5fc358f17310378","user":{"login":"jkrumbiegel","id":22495855,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIyNDk1ODU1","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/22495855?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jkrumbiegel","html_url":"https://github.com/jkrumbiegel","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jkrumbiegel/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jkrumbiegel/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jkrumbiegel/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jkrumbiegel/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jkrumbiegel/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jkrumbiegel/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jkrumbiegel/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jkrumbiegel/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jkrumbiegel/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"I would just explain here briefly what it does, otherwise people have to context switch. +## Execution latency, loading and precompiling time
+
+### Reducing time to first plot etc.
+
+The first time a julia function is called it will be compiled. The [`@time`](@ref) macro family illustrates this.
+
+```
+julia> foo() = rand(2,2) * rand(2,2)
+foo (generic function with 1 method)
+
+julia> @time @eval foo();
+  0.252395 seconds (1.12 M allocations: 56.178 MiB, 2.93% gc time, 98.12% compilation time)
+
+julia> @time @eval foo();
+  0.000156 seconds (63 allocations: 2.453 KiB)
+```
+
+Note that `@time @eval ...` form is explained in the [`@time`](@ref) documentation.
+
+When developing a package, it can be helpful to cache this compilation in the package caches that are "precompiled"
+during the `Pkg` installation process or before package load. To precompile package code effectively, it's recommended
+to use [`PrecompileTools.jl`](https://julialang.github.io/PrecompileTools.jl/stable/) to run a "precompile workload" during
+precompilation time that is representative of typical package usage, which will cache the native compiled code into the
+package `pkgimage` cache, greatly reducing "time to first ..." (often referred to as TTFX) for such usage.
+
+
+### Reducing package loading time
+

+## Execution latency, loading and precompiling time
+
+### Reducing time to first plot etc.
+
+The first time a julia function is called it will be compiled. The [`@time`](@ref) macro family illustrates this.
+
+```
+julia> foo() = rand(2,2) * rand(2,2)
+foo (generic function with 1 method)
+
+julia> @time @eval foo();
+  0.252395 seconds (1.12 M allocations: 56.178 MiB, 2.93% gc time, 98.12% compilation time)
+
+julia> @time @eval foo();
+  0.000156 seconds (63 allocations: 2.453 KiB)
+```
+
+Note that `@time @eval ...` form is explained in the [`@time`](@ref) documentation.
+
+When developing a package, it can be helpful to cache this compilation in the package caches that are "precompiled"
+during the `Pkg` installation process or before package load. To precompile package code effectively, it's recommended
+to use [`PrecompileTools.jl`](https://julialang.github.io/PrecompileTools.jl/stable/) to run a "precompile workload" during
+precompilation time that is representative of typical package usage, which will cache the native compiled code into the
+package `pkgimage` cache, greatly reducing "time to first ..." (often referred to as TTFX) for such usage.
+
+
+### Reducing package loading time
+
+When keeping the time taken to load the package down is usually helpful.
+General good practice for package developers includes:
+
+1. Balancing the need to depending on packages for functionality vs. their load times, including stdlibs which on newer
+   julia versions may not be loaded already as stdlibs are no longer always included in the default  end
+
+"""
+    @check_allocs_call ignore_throw=true (function call)
+
+Calls `check_allocs(func, types; ignore_throw)` where `types` is a tuple of the types of the arguments
+passed to `func` in the function call expression that `@check_allocs_call` is applied to.
+For example, `@check_allocs_call 1 + 2.0` is equivalent to `check_allocs(+, (Int, Float64))`.
+"""
+macro content is hard to deal with. This contribution aims to drastically improve that.

Closes #41791, closes #41435, and I think also resolves #40228 and resolves #28690.

## Current holdups

- Review and acceptance

## New public API

- `StyledString`
- `StyledChar`
- `Base.Face`
- `addface!`
- `@S_str`
- `styledstring`

should anything else be added?

## Motivation

Julia already treats the REPL experience better than most other languages out there (🐍), and I think it's great how often I see the REPL as a selling point of Julia.

However... styling is hard. Across `Base` and the package ecosystem we have a painful mixture of raw ANSI codes and an unsightly pile of `printstyled` lines. This isn't just ugly, this leads to reoccurring issues with incorrectly computed string lengths/padding and unterminated styling codes (e.g. #37568 and #45521).

We can do better than this. Rethinking our approach to styled content allows us to do away with this class of bugs/headaches altogether, and gain a number of shiny new features at the same time.

Hy hope is that the capabilities introduced by this PR will lead to a much more robust approach to string styling, and by making it easier to produce well-styled content even more beautiful REPL experiences across the board.

## Rundown of changes

An `StyledString` type is introduced that effectively sits on top of other string types. It wraps another `AbstractString` but then separately stores a list of attributes applied to particular regions of the wrapped string. Regions are represented by a `UnitRange{Int}`, and attributes as a `Pair{Symbol, Any}`.

This effectively creates "content" and "attributes" layers, allowing both to be handled much better (conveniently and robustly) than when mixed together. Attributes can be *`Any`thing*, which allows for more than just styling information, e.g. hyperlinks, or other data which makes sense to attach to a region of a string like source location information. Really `StyledString` should be called `PropertizedString`, but I think that's much less catchy 😛.

An `StyledChar` type is also added, which is a mirror of `AbstractString`, just wrapping an `AbstractChar` instead.

To handle styling information, a new type is added to contain all possibly relevant styling information — `Face`. This is very much inspired by https://www.gnu.org/software/emacs/manual/html_node/elisp/Faces.html, as I have found this system to work rather well in practice (why invent a new approach when there's a lovely battle-tested one we can be inspired by? 😉).

The main way faces work is by having a global `Dics{Symbol, Face}` of face names. This has a number of advantages:
- It makes it easy to reference faces by their name
- Packages can easily add new faces
- It makes it possible for faces to inherit from other faces (e.g. have a `:julia_help_prompt` face that inherits from `:julia_prompt`)

Conversion functions to accept and load face specifications from a `Dict{String, Any}` are also added. By loading `~/.julia/config/faces.toml` this resolves #41435.

To handle printing faces well, we need to inquire about the terminal a fair bit more, and so I've replaced `tcap` with a terminfo parser implemented in accordance with `term(5)`.

Lastly, we have a string macro (`@S_str`) that was rather nasty to write, but is very convenient.

#### Currently unimplemented

- `replace`
- property removal

## Usage examples

The example from the `@S_str` docs

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235492934-d3299633-36fe-4a21-8321-83e24d8c4a6f.png)

Easy nested styling

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235493196-753f50e4-3acc-4821-9146-8c1aef5e6cd1.png)

No more issues with unterminated ANSI codes

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235494848-eccefbda-f943-4c1d-85a0-dbd2297b9217.png)

String functions operate on the unstyled content, while preserving styling where possible

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235496767-1d537db9-befc-4ee6-b482-e91778e32ceb.png)

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235496290-d3b6bb79-9c33-4dce-bf6f-b20d696cc5b2.png)

Styled text will actually automatically fall back to the un-styled form when printed to an `io` with `:color != true`.

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235496566-330a621d-e0d9-4b36-8714-e9d225e89ef7.png)

Incrementally styling a string, and changing face definitions on-the-fly (note: this uses currently "private API" functions)

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235499614-ce8d5aca-a561-4280-979e-d6c176efd1df.png)

## Fancier usage examples

The result of the example "banner using `S"..."`" commit

![image](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/assets/20903656/1e5cdf8a-4e3e-48ef-ae94-a0850c484270)

A fancier `^R` implementation, that's been implemented using `StyledString`s and `JuliaSyntax` that would be a massive pain to do via `printstyled` et al.

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235492830-4e0476d1-0716-48df-be8a-b1f26fe9b53d.png)

content is hard to deal with. This contribution aims to drastically improve that.

Closes #41791, closes #41435, and I think also resolves #40228 and resolves #28690.

## Current holdups

- Review and acceptance

## New public API

- `StyledString`
- `StyledChar`
- `Base.Face`
- `addface!`
- `@S_str`
- `styledstring`

should anything else be added?

## Motivation

Julia already treats the REPL experience better than most other languages out there (🐍), and I think it's great how often I see the REPL as a selling point of Julia.

However... styling is hard. Across `Base` and the package ecosystem we have a painful mixture of raw ANSI codes and an unsightly pile of `printstyled` lines. This isn't just ugly, this leads to reoccurring issues with incorrectly computed string lengths/padding and unterminated styling codes (e.g. #37568 and #45521).

We can do better than this. Rethinking our approach to styled content allows us to do away with this class of bugs/headaches altogether, and gain a number of shiny new features at the same time.

Hy hope is that the capabilities introduced by this PR will lead to a much more robust approach to string styling, and by making it easier to produce well-styled content even more beautiful REPL experiences across the board.

## Rundown of changes

An `StyledString` type is introduced that effectively sits on top of other string types. It wraps another `AbstractString` but then separately stores a list of attributes applied to particular regions of the wrapped string. Regions are represented by a `UnitRange{Int}`, and attributes as a `Pair{Symbol, Any}`.

This effectively creates "content" and "attributes" layers, allowing both to be handled much better (conveniently and robustly) than when mixed together. Attributes can be *`Any`thing*, which allows for more than just styling information, e.g. hyperlinks, or other data which makes sense to attach to a region of a string like source location information. Really `StyledString` should be called `PropertizedString`, but I think that's much less catchy 😛.

An `StyledChar` type is also added, which is a mirror of `AbstractString`, just wrapping an `AbstractChar` instead.

To handle styling information, a new type is added to contain all possibly relevant styling information — `Face`. This is very much inspired by https://www.gnu.org/software/emacs/manual/html_node/elisp/Faces.html, as I have found this system to work rather well in practice (why invent a new approach when there's a lovely battle-tested one we can be inspired by? 😉).

The main way faces work is by having a global `Dics{Symbol, Face}` of face names. This has a number of advantages:
- It makes it easy to reference faces by their name
- Packages can easily add new faces
- It makes it possible for faces to inherit from other faces (e.g. have a `:julia_help_prompt` face that inherits from `:julia_prompt`)

Conversion functions to accept and load face specifications from a `Dict{String, Any}` are also added. By loading `~/.julia/config/faces.toml` this resolves #41435.

To handle printing faces well, we need to inquire about the terminal a fair bit more, and so I've replaced `tcap` with a terminfo parser implemented in accordance with `term(5)`.

Lastly, we have a string macro (`@S_str`) that was rather nasty to write, but is very convenient.

#### Currently unimplemented

- `replace`
- property removal

## Usage examples

The example from the `@S_str` docs

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235492934-d3299633-36fe-4a21-8321-83e24d8c4a6f.png)

Easy nested styling

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235493196-753f50e4-3acc-4821-9146-8c1aef5e6cd1.png)

No more issues with unterminated ANSI codes

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235494848-eccefbda-f943-4c1d-85a0-dbd2297b9217.png)

String functions operate on the unstyled content, while preserving styling where possible

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235496767-1d537db9-befc-4ee6-b482-e91778e32ceb.png)

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235496290-d3b6bb79-9c33-4dce-bf6f-b20d696cc5b2.png)

Styled text will actually automatically fall back to the un-styled form when printed to an `io` with `:color != true`.

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235496566-330a621d-e0d9-4b36-8714-e9d225e89ef7.png)

Incrementally styling a string, and changing face definitions on-the-fly (note: this uses currently "private API" functions)

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235499614-ce8d5aca-a561-4280-979e-d6c176efd1df.png)

## Fancier usage examples

The result of the example "banner using `S"..."`" commit

![image](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/assets/20903656/1e5cdf8a-4e3e-48ef-ae94-a0850c484270)

A fancier `^R` implementation, that's been implemented using `StyledString`s and `JuliaSyntax` that would be a massive pain to do via `printstyled` et al.

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235492830-4e0476d1-0716-48df-be8a-b1f26fe9b53d.png)

With very little work, docstring previews could look like this

![image](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/assets/20903656/f546de5c-e2ec-4ba8-bcf7-d2ff714bb928)

content is hard to deal with. This contribution aims to drastically improve that.

Closes #41791, closes #41435, and I think also resolves #40228 and resolves #28690.

## Current holdups

- Finalising the naming

## New public API

- `TaggedString`
- `TaggedChar`
- `taggedstring`
- `Base.Face`
- `addface!`
- `@S_str`

## Motivation

Julia already treats the REPL experience better than most other languages out there (🐍), and I think it's great how often I see the REPL as a selling point of Julia.

However... styling is hard. Across `Base` and the package ecosystem we have a painful mixture of raw ANSI codes and an unsightly pile of `printstyled` lines. This isn't just ugly, this leads to reoccurring issues with incorrectly computed string lengths/padding and unterminated styling codes (e.g. #37568 and #45521).

We can do better than this. Rethinking our approach to styled content allows us to do away with this class of bugs/headaches altogether, and gain a number of shiny new features at the same time.

Hy hope is that the capabilities introduced by this PR will lead to a much more robust approach to string styling, and by making it easier to produce well-styled content even more beautiful REPL experiences across the board.

## Rundown of changes

An `StyledString` type is introduced that effectively sits on top of other string types. It wraps another `AbstractString` but then separately stores a list of attributes applied to particular regions of the wrapped string. Regions are represented by a `UnitRange{Int}`, and attributes as a `Pair{Symbol, Any}`.

This effectively creates "content" and "attributes" layers, allowing both to be handled much better (conveniently and robustly) than when mixed together. Attributes can be *`Any`thing*, which allows for more than just styling information, e.g. hyperlinks, or other data which makes sense to attach to a region of a string like source location information. Really `StyledString` should be called `PropertizedString`, but I think that's much less catchy 😛.

An `StyledChar` type is also added, which is a mirror of `AbstractString`, just wrapping an `AbstractChar` instead.

To handle styling information, a new type is added to contain all possibly relevant styling information — `Face`. This is very much inspired by https://www.gnu.org/software/emacs/manual/html_node/elisp/Faces.html, as I have found this system to work rather well in practice (why invent a new approach when there's a lovely battle-tested one we can be inspired by? 😉).

The main way faces work is by having a global `Dics{Symbol, Face}` of face names. This has a number of advantages:
- It makes it easy to reference faces by their name
- Packages can easily add new faces
- It makes it possible for faces to inherit from other faces (e.g. have a `:julia_help_prompt` face that inherits from `:julia_prompt`)

Conversion functions to accept and load face specifications from a `Dict{String, Any}` are also added. By loading `~/.julia/config/faces.toml` this resolves #41435.

To handle printing faces well, we need to inquire about the terminal a fair bit more, and so I've replaced `tcap` with a terminfo parser implemented in accordance with `term(5)`.

Lastly, we have a string macro (`@S_str`) that was rather nasty to write, but is very convenient.

#### Currently unimplemented

- `replace`
- property removal

## Usage examples

The example from the `@S_str` docs

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235492934-d3299633-36fe-4a21-8321-83e24d8c4a6f.png)

Easy nested styling

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235493196-753f50e4-3acc-4821-9146-8c1aef5e6cd1.png)

No more issues with unterminated ANSI codes

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235494848-eccefbda-f943-4c1d-85a0-dbd2297b9217.png)

String functions operate on the unstyled content, while preserving styling where possible

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235496767-1d537db9-befc-4ee6-b482-e91778e32ceb.png)

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235496290-d3b6bb79-9c33-4dce-bf6f-b20d696cc5b2.png)

Styled text will actually automatically fall back to the un-styled form when printed to an `io` with `:color != true`.

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235496566-330a621d-e0d9-4b36-8714-e9d225e89ef7.png)

Incrementally styling a string, and changing face definitions on-the-fly (note: this uses currently "private API" functions)

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235499614-ce8d5aca-a561-4280-979e-d6c176efd1df.png)

## Fancier usage examples

The result of the example "banner using `S"..."`" commit

![image](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/assets/20903656/1e5cdf8a-4e3e-48ef-ae94-a0850c484270)

A fancier `^R` implementation, that's been implemented using `StyledString`s and `JuliaSyntax` that would be a massive pain to do via `printstyled` et al.

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235492830-4e0476d1-0716-48df-be8a-b1f26fe9b53d.png)

With very little work, docstring previews could look like this

![image](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/assets/20903656/f546de5c-e2ec-4ba8-bcf7-d2ff714bb928)

A real conflict only appears if this is default-exported, though. As long as it isn't, I don't see a problem with choosing the shorter name. Attributes can be *`Any`thing*, which allows for more than just styling information, e.g. hyperlinks, or other data which makes sense to attach to a region of a string like source location information. Really `StyledString` should be called `PropertizedString`, but I think that's much less catchy 😛. An `StyledChar` type is also added, which is a mirror of `AbstractString`, just wrapping an `AbstractChar` instead. To handle styling information, a new type is added to contain all possibly relevant styling information — `Face`. This is very much inspired by https://www.gnu.org/software/emacs/manual/html_node/elisp/Faces.html, as I have found this system to work rather well in practice (why invent a new approach when there's a lovely battle-tested one we can be inspired by? 😉). The main way faces work is by having a global `Dics{Symbol, Face}` of face names. This has a number of advantages: - It makes it easy to reference faces by their name - Packages can easily add new faces - It makes it possible for faces to inherit from other faces (e.g. have a `:julia_help_prompt` face that inherits from `:julia_prompt`) Conversion functions to accept and load face specifications from a `Dict{String, Any}` are also added. By loading `~/.julia/config/faces.toml` this resolves #41435. To handle printing faces well, we need to inquire about the terminal a fair bit more, and so I've replaced `tcap` with a terminfo parser implemented in accordance with `term(5)`. Lastly, we have a string macro (`@S_str`) that was rather nasty to write, but is very convenient. #### Currently unimplemented - `replace` - property removal ## Usage examples The example from the Closes #41791, closes #41435, and I think also resolves #40228 and resolves #28690. ## Current holdups - Finalising the naming ## New public API - `TaggedString` - `TaggedChar` - `taggedstring` - `Base.Face` - `addface!` - `@S_str` ## Motivation Julia already treats the REPL experience better than most other languages out there (🐍), and I think it's great how often I see the REPL as a selling point of Julia. However... styling is hard. Across `Base` and the package ecosystem we have a painful mixture of raw ANSI codes and an unsightly pile of `printstyled` lines. This isn't just ugly, this leads to reoccurring issues with incorrectly computed string lengths/padding and unterminated styling codes (e.g. #37568 and #45521). We can do better than this. Rethinking our approach to styled content allows us to do away with this class of bugs/headaches altogether, and gain a number of shiny new features at the same time. Hy hope is that the capabilities introduced by this PR will lead to a much more robust approach to string styling, and by making it easier to produce well-styled content even more beautiful REPL experiences across the board. ## Rundown of changes An `StyledString` type is introduced that effectively sits on top of other string types. It wraps another `AbstractString` but then separately stores a list of attributes applied to particular regions of the wrapped string. Regions are represented by a `UnitRange{Int}`, and attributes as a `Pair{Symbol, Any}`. This effectively creates "content" and "attributes" layers, allowing both to be handled much better (conveniently and robustly) than when mixed together. Attributes can be *`Any`thing*, which allows for more than just styling information, e.g. hyperlinks, or other data which makes sense to attach to a region of a string like source location information. Really `StyledString` should be called `PropertizedString`, but I think that's much less catchy 😛. An `StyledChar` type is also added, which is a mirror of `AbstractString`, just wrapping an `AbstractChar` instead. To handle styling information, a new type is added to contain all possibly relevant styling information — `Face`. This is very much inspired by https://www.gnu.org/software/emacs/manual/html_node/elisp/Faces.html, as I have found this system to work rather well in practice (why invent a new approach when there's a lovely battle-tested one we can be inspired by? 😉). The main way faces work is by having a global `Dics{Symbol, Face}` of face names. This has a number of advantages: - It makes it easy to reference faces by their name - Packages can easily add new faces - It makes it possible for faces to inherit from other faces (e.g. have a `:julia_help_prompt` face that inherits from `:julia_prompt`) Conversion functions to accept and load face specifications from a `Dict{String, Any}` are also added. By loading `~/.julia/config/faces.toml` this resolves #41435. To handle printing faces well, we need to inquire about the terminal a fair bit more, and so I've replaced `tcap` with a terminfo parser implemented in accordance with `term(5)`. Lastly, we have a string macro (`@S_str`) that was rather nasty to write, but is very convenient. #### Currently unimplemented - `replace` - property removal ## Usage examples The example from the Closes #41791, closes #41435, and I think also resolves #40228 and resolves #28690. ## Current holdups - Finalising the naming ## New public API - `TaggedString` - `TaggedChar` - `taggedstring` - `Base.Face` - `addface!` - `@S_str` ## Motivation Julia already treats the REPL experience better than most other languages out there (🐍), and I think it's great how often I see the REPL as a selling point of Julia. However... styling is hard. Across `Base` and the package ecosystem we have a painful mixture of raw ANSI codes and an unsightly pile of `printstyled` lines. This isn't just ugly, this leads to reoccurring issues with incorrectly computed string lengths/padding and unterminated styling codes (e.g. #37568 and #45521). We can do better than this. Rethinking our approach to styled content allows us to do away with this class of bugs/headaches altogether, and gain a number of shiny new features at the same time. Hy hope is that the capabilities introduced by this PR will lead to a much more robust approach to string styling, and by making it easier to produce well-styled content even more beautiful REPL experiences across the board. ## Rundown of changes An `StyledString` type is introduced that effectively sits on top of other string types. It wraps another `AbstractString` but then separately stores a list of attributes applied to particular regions of the wrapped string. Regions are represented by a `UnitRange{Int}`, and attributes as a `Pair{Symbol, Any}`. This effectively creates "content" and "attributes" layers, allowing both to be handled much better (conveniently and robustly) than when mixed together. Attributes can be *`Any`thing*, which allows for more than just styling information, e.g. hyperlinks, or other data which makes sense to attach to a region of a string like source location information. Really `StyledString` should be called `PropertizedString`, but I think that's much less catchy 😛. An `StyledChar` type is also added, which is a mirror of `AbstractString`, just wrapping an `AbstractChar` instead. To handle styling information, a new type is added to contain all possibly relevant styling information — `Face`. This is very much inspired by https://www.gnu.org/software/emacs/manual/html_node/elisp/Faces.html, as I have found this system to work rather well in practice (why invent a new approach when there's a lovely battle-tested one we can be inspired by? 😉). The main way faces work is by having a global `Dics{Symbol, Face}` of face names. This has a number of advantages: - It makes it easy to reference faces by their name - Packages can easily add new faces - It makes it possible for faces to inherit from other faces (e.g. have a `:julia_help_prompt` face that inherits from `:julia_prompt`) Conversion functions to accept and load face specifications from a `Dict{String, Any}` are also added. By loading `~/.julia/config/faces.toml` this resolves #41435. To handle printing faces well, we need to inquire about the terminal a fair bit more, and so I've replaced `tcap` with a terminfo parser implemented in accordance with `term(5)`. Lastly, we have a string macro (`@S_str`) that was rather nasty to write, but is very convenient. #### Currently unimplemented - `replace` - property removal ## Usage examples The example from the content is hard to deal with. This contribution aims to drastically improve that.

Closes #41791, closes #41435, and I think also resolves #40228 and resolves #28690.

## Current holdups

- Finalising the naming

## New public API

- `TaggedString`
- `TaggedChar`
- `taggedstring`
- `Base.Face`
- `addface!`
- `@S_str`

## Motivation

Julia already treats the REPL experience better than most other languages out there (🐍), and I think it's great how often I see the REPL as a selling point of Julia.

However... styling is hard. Across `Base` and the package ecosystem we have a painful mixture of raw ANSI codes and an unsightly pile of `printstyled` lines. This isn't just ugly, this leads to reoccurring issues with incorrectly computed string lengths/padding and unterminated styling codes (e.g. #37568 and #45521).

We can do better than this. Rethinking our approach to styled content allows us to do away with this class of bugs/headaches altogether, and gain a number of shiny new features at the same time.

Hy hope is that the capabilities introduced by this PR will lead to a much more robust approach to string styling, and by making it easier to produce well-styled content even more beautiful REPL experiences across the board.

## Rundown of changes

An `StyledString` type is introduced that effectively sits on top of other string types. It wraps another `AbstractString` but then separately stores a list of attributes applied to particular regions of the wrapped string. Regions are represented by a `UnitRange{Int}`, and attributes as a `Pair{Symbol, Any}`.

This effectively creates "content" and "attributes" layers, allowing both to be handled much better (conveniently and robustly) than when mixed together. Attributes can be *`Any`thing*, which allows for more than just styling information, e.g. hyperlinks, or other data which makes sense to attach to a region of a string like source location information. Really `StyledString` should be called `PropertizedString`, but I think that's much less catchy 😛.

An `StyledChar` type is also added, which is a mirror of `AbstractString`, just wrapping an `AbstractChar` instead.

To handle styling information, a new type is added to contain all possibly relevant styling information — `Face`. This is very much inspired by https://www.gnu.org/software/emacs/manual/html_node/elisp/Faces.html, as I have found this system to work rather well in practice (why invent a new approach when there's a lovely battle-tested one we can be inspired by? 😉).

The main way faces work is by having a global `Dics{Symbol, Face}` of face names. This has a number of advantages:
- It makes it easy to reference faces by their name
- Packages can easily add new faces
- It makes it possible for faces to inherit from other faces (e.g. have a `:julia_help_prompt` face that inherits from `:julia_prompt`)

Conversion functions to accept and load face specifications from a `Dict{String, Any}` are also added. By loading `~/.julia/config/faces.toml` this resolves #41435.

To handle printing faces well, we need to inquire about the terminal a fair bit more, and so I've replaced `tcap` with a terminfo parser implemented in accordance with `term(5)`.

Lastly, we have a string macro (`@S_str`) that was rather nasty to write, but is very convenient.

#### Currently unimplemented

- `replace`
- property removal

## Usage examples

The example from the `@S_str` docs

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235492934-d3299633-36fe-4a21-8321-83e24d8c4a6f.png)

Easy nested styling

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235493196-753f50e4-3acc-4821-9146-8c1aef5e6cd1.png)

No more issues with unterminated ANSI codes

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235494848-eccefbda-f943-4c1d-85a0-dbd2297b9217.png)

String functions operate on the unstyled content, while preserving styling where possible

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235496767-1d537db9-befc-4ee6-b482-e91778e32ceb.png)

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235496290-d3b6bb79-9c33-4dce-bf6f-b20d696cc5b2.png)

Styled text will actually automatically fall back to the un-styled form when printed to an `io` with `:color != true`.

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235496566-330a621d-e0d9-4b36-8714-e9d225e89ef7.png)

Incrementally styling a string, and changing face definitions on-the-fly (note: this uses currently "private API" functions)

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235499614-ce8d5aca-a561-4280-979e-d6c176efd1df.png)

## Fancier usage examples

The result of the example "banner using `S"..."`" commit

![image](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/assets/20903656/1e5cdf8a-4e3e-48ef-ae94-a0850c484270)

A fancier `^R` implementation, that's been implemented using `StyledString`s and `JuliaSyntax` that would be a massive pain to do via `printstyled` et al.

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235492830-4e0476d1-0716-48df-be8a-b1f26fe9b53d.png)

With very little work, docstring previews could look like this

![image](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/assets/20903656/f546de5c-e2ec-4ba8-bcf7-d2ff714bb928)

Probably just throwing a more informative error suggesting what types are appropriate there

> Hmm, we could probably indicate the start of the terminated styles.

Would be worth it I think because that's one of the most common errors you're going to have with this I think, when you style some strings that happen to have curly braces.

> I kind of like the idea that using this macro the only thing that will error is undefined variables / invalid interpolations.

Have to disagree there :) While warnings seem friendlier at first, I think they bite you later when things run on CI for example. These invalid syntax errors are things that should mostly be trivial to fix for a user, so the benefit of "hey we're doing something half-correct for you so don't have to stop what you were doing" is not that large.

> Because loss of styling is an aesthetic issue, and so IMO lesser than a "different string" -type issue, I'm feel the "skip faces that don't exist" behavior is reasonable.

I would say a terminal swallowing color info because of some setting is an aesthetic issue, a face that you expect to be there not being there is not. Unless you anticipate your system should regularly have the situation that faces are disappearing, and it would be too annoying if things kept throwing errors, I think this classifies as a bug. Think about how much trouble the Documenter ecosystem had with half-broken docs because the thinking was, well an unreachable URL or a failing example is not a reason to completely break the build, right?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1761058056/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

content is hard to deal with. This contribution aims to drastically improve that.

Closes #41791, closes #41435, and I think also resolves #40228 and resolves #28690.

## Current holdups

- Finalising the naming

## New public API

- `TaggedString`
- `TaggedChar`
- `taggedstring`
- `Base.Face`
- `addface!`
- `@S_str`

## Motivation

Julia already treats the REPL experience better than most other languages out there (🐍), and I think it's great how often I see the REPL as a selling point of Julia.

However... styling is hard. Across `Base` and the package ecosystem we have a painful mixture of raw ANSI codes and an unsightly pile of `printstyled` lines. This isn't just ugly, this leads to reoccurring issues with incorrectly computed string lengths/padding and unterminated styling codes (e.g. #37568 and #45521).

We can do better than this. Rethinking our approach to styled content allows us to do away with this class of bugs/headaches altogether, and gain a number of shiny new features at the same time.

Hy hope is that the capabilities introduced by this PR will lead to a much more robust approach to string styling, and by making it easier to produce well-styled content even more beautiful REPL experiences across the board.

## Rundown of changes

An `StyledString` type is introduced that effectively sits on top of other string types. It wraps another `AbstractString` but then separately stores a list of attributes applied to particular regions of the wrapped string. Regions are represented by a `UnitRange{Int}`, and attributes as a `Pair{Symbol, Any}`.

This effectively creates "content" and "attributes" layers, allowing both to be handled much better (conveniently and robustly) than when mixed together. Attributes can be *`Any`thing*, which allows for more than just styling information, e.g. hyperlinks, or other data which makes sense to attach to a region of a string like source location information. Really `StyledString` should be called `PropertizedString`, but I think that's much less catchy 😛.

An `StyledChar` type is also added, which is a mirror of `AbstractString`, just wrapping an `AbstractChar` instead.

To handle styling information, a new type is added to contain all possibly relevant styling information — `Face`. This is very much inspired by https://www.gnu.org/software/emacs/manual/html_node/elisp/Faces.html, as I have found this system to work rather well in practice (why invent a new approach when there's a lovely battle-tested one we can be inspired by? 😉).

The main way faces work is by having a global `Dics{Symbol, Face}` of face names. This has a number of advantages:
- It makes it easy to reference faces by their name
- Packages can easily add new faces
- It makes it possible for faces to inherit from other faces (e.g. have a `:julia_help_prompt` face that inherits from `:julia_prompt`)

Conversion functions to accept and load face specifications from a `Dict{String, Any}` are also added. By loading `~/.julia/config/faces.toml` this resolves #41435.

To handle printing faces well, we need to inquire about the terminal a fair bit more, and so I've replaced `tcap` with a terminfo parser implemented in accordance with `term(5)`.

Lastly, we have a string macro (`@S_str`) that was rather nasty to write, but is very convenient.

#### Currently unimplemented

- `replace`
- property removal

## Usage examples

The example from the `@S_str` docs

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235492934-d3299633-36fe-4a21-8321-83e24d8c4a6f.png)

Easy nested styling

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235493196-753f50e4-3acc-4821-9146-8c1aef5e6cd1.png)

No more issues with unterminated ANSI codes

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235494848-eccefbda-f943-4c1d-85a0-dbd2297b9217.png)

String functions operate on the unstyled content, while preserving styling where possible

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235496767-1d537db9-befc-4ee6-b482-e91778e32ceb.png)

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235496290-d3b6bb79-9c33-4dce-bf6f-b20d696cc5b2.png)

Styled text will actually automatically fall back to the un-styled form when printed to an `io` with `:color != true`.

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235496566-330a621d-e0d9-4b36-8714-e9d225e89ef7.png)

Incrementally styling a string, and changing face definitions on-the-fly (note: this uses currently "private API" functions)

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235499614-ce8d5aca-a561-4280-979e-d6c176efd1df.png)

## Fancier usage examples

The result of the example "banner using `S"..."`" commit

![image](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/assets/20903656/1e5cdf8a-4e3e-48ef-ae94-a0850c484270)

A fancier `^R` implementation, that's been implemented using `StyledString`s and `JuliaSyntax` that would be a massive pain to do via `printstyled` et al.

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235492830-4e0476d1-0716-48df-be8a-b1f26fe9b53d.png)

With very little work, docstring previews could look like this

![image](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/assets/20903656/f546de5c-e2ec-4ba8-bcf7-d2ff714bb928)

With this "soft-error" (nothing thrown, you just don't see styling from non-existent named faces), approach this can be done easily. Otherwise... I guess you could resort to a package extension that changes a global variable saying what faces should be used on load? But that seems uglier to me.

I still think that mechanism is kind of confusing, and I know there has been discussion on the `Faces` mechanism in the past. But I don't think it's good if I do styling in `Makie` where I use a face called `diffeq_custom_face` or so and leave it up to chance whether that's ever loaded or not, or if it changes in ways that I couldn't predict. This is basically the problem with the global faces storage, you cannot really make predictions about it. Rather than concluding from this that missing faces must be ignored when rendering, I would question the underlying reference mechanism again.

As far as I can tell, currently faces are special in that they are resolved as entries in the global dict when encountered in the styling macro. Couldn't we change that to a function call instead that resolves this in the current module? So `S"{someface:text}" would resolve to `CurrentModule._get_face_someface()` which the module owner can define somehow. And if they wanted to use other faces they could load them from other modules first maybe? Probably this mechanism would need convenience macros so you don't actually have to write `_get_face_someface` and so that there can't be name clashes. But I think if you rely on a face from another package, that should be handled like any other thing that you depend on, with a clear failure mode.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1764160013/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

merge"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":55,"created_at":"2023-05-01T18:01:45Z","updated_at":"2023-09-03T12:27:53Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/49586","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/49586","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/49586.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/49586.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"Styled content is hard to deal with. This contribution aims to drastically improve that.

## Current holdups

- Lack of feedback
- Lack of documentation
- Lack of tests

## New public API

- `StyledString`
- `StyledChar`
- `Base.Face`
- `addface!`
- `@S_str`
- `styledstring`

should anything else be added?

## Motivation

Julia already treats the REPL experience better than most other languages out there (🐍), and I think it's great how often I see the REPL as a selling point of Julia.

However... styling is hard. Across `Base` and the package ecosystem we have a painful mixture of raw ANSI codes and an unsightly pile of `printstyled` lines. This isn't just ugly, this leads to reoccurring issues with incorrectly computed string lengths/padding and unterminated styling codes (e.g. #37568 and #45521).

We can do better than this. Rethinking our approach to styled content allows us to do away with this class of bugs/headaches altogether, and gain a number of shiny new features at the same time.

Hy hope is that the capabilities introduced by this PR will lead to a much more robust approach to string styling, and by making it easier to produce well-styled content even more beautiful REPL experiences across the board.

## Rundown of changes

An `StyledString` type is introduced that effectively sits on top of other string types. It wraps another `AbstractString` but then separately stores a list of attributes applied to particular regions of the wrapped string. Regions are represented by a `UnitRange{Int}`, and attributes as a `Pair{Symbol, Any}`.

This effectively creates "content" and "attributes" layers, allowing both to be handled much better (conveniently and robustly) than when mixed together. Attributes can be *`Any`thing*, which allows for more than just styling information, e.g. hyperlinks, or other data which makes sense to attach to a region of a string like source location information. Really `StyledString` should be called `PropertizedString`, but I think that's much less catchy 😛.

An `StyledChar` type is also added, which is a mirror of `AbstractString`, just wrapping an `AbstractChar` instead.

To handle styling information, a new type is added to contain all possibly relevant styling information — `Face`. This is very much inspired by https://www.gnu.org/software/emacs/manual/html_node/elisp/Faces.html, as I have found this system to work rather well in practice (why invent a new approach when there's a lovely battle-tested one we can be inspired by? 😉).

The main way faces work is by having a global `Dics{Symbol, Face}` of face names. This has a number of advantages:
- It makes it easy to reference faces by their name
- Packages can easily add new faces
- It makes it possible for faces to inherit from other faces (e.g. have a `:julia_help_prompt` face that inherits from `:julia_prompt`)

Conversion functions to accept and load face specifications from a `Dict{String, Any}` are also added. By loading `~/.julia/config/faces.toml` this resolves #41435.

To handle printing faces well, we need to inquire about the terminal a fair bit more, and so I've replaced `tcap` with a terminfo parser implemented in accordance with `term(5)`.

Lastly, we have a string macro (`@S_str`) that was rather nasty to write, but is very convenient.

#### Currently unimplemented

- `replace`
- property removal

## Usage examples

The example from the `@S_str` docs

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235492934-d3299633-36fe-4a21-8321-83e24d8c4a6f.png)

Easy nested styling

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235493196-753f50e4-3acc-4821-9146-8c1aef5e6cd1.png)

No more issues with unterminated ANSI codes

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235494848-eccefbda-f943-4c1d-85a0-dbd2297b9217.png)

String functions operate on the unstyled content, while preserving styling where possible

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235496767-1d537db9-befc-4ee6-b482-e91778e32ceb.png)

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235496290-d3b6bb79-9c33-4dce-bf6f-b20d696cc5b2.png)

Styled text will actually automatically fall back to the un-styled form when printed to an `io` with `:color != true`.

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235496566-330a621d-e0d9-4b36-8714-e9d225e89ef7.png)

Incrementally styling a string, and changing face definitions on-the-fly (note: this uses currently "private API" functions)

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235499614-ce8d5aca-a561-4280-979e-d6c176efd1df.png)

## Fancier usage examples

The result of the example "banner using `S"..."`" commit

![image](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/assets/20903656/1e5cdf8a-4e3e-48ef-ae94-a0850c484270)

A fancier `^R` implementation, that's been implemented using `StyledString`s and `JuliaSyntax` that would be a massive pain to do via `printstyled` et al.

merge"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":99,"created_at":"2023-05-01T18:01:45Z","updated_at":"2023-09-28T18:15:15Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/49586","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/49586","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/49586.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/49586.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"Styled content is hard to deal with. This contribution aims to drastically improve that.

Closes #41791, closes #41435, and I think also resolves #40228 and resolves #28690.

## Current holdups

- Review and acceptance

## New public API

- `StyledString`
- `StyledChar`
- `Base.Face`
- `addface!`
- `@S_str`
- `styledstring`

should anything else be added?

## Motivation

Julia already treats the REPL experience better than most other languages out there (🐍), and I think it's great how often I see the REPL as a selling point of Julia.

However... styling is hard. Across `Base` and the package ecosystem we have a painful mixture of raw ANSI codes and an unsightly pile of `printstyled` lines. This isn't just ugly, this leads to reoccurring issues with incorrectly computed string lengths/padding and unterminated styling codes (e.g. #37568 and #45521).

We can do better than this. Rethinking our approach to styled content allows us to do away with this class of bugs/headaches altogether, and gain a number of shiny new features at the same time.

Hy hope is that the capabilities introduced by this PR will lead to a much more robust approach to string styling, and by making it easier to produce well-styled content even more beautiful REPL experiences across the board.

## Rundown of changes

An `StyledString` type is introduced that effectively sits on top of other string types. It wraps another `AbstractString` but then separately stores a list of attributes applied to particular regions of the wrapped string. Regions are represented by a `UnitRange{Int}`, and attributes as a `Pair{Symbol, Any}`.

This effectively creates "content" and "attributes" layers, allowing both to be handled much better (conveniently and robustly) than when mixed together. Attributes can be *`Any`thing*, which allows for more than just styling information, e.g. hyperlinks, or other data which makes sense to attach to a region of a string like source location information. Really `StyledString` should be called `PropertizedString`, but I think that's much less catchy 😛.

An `StyledChar` type is also added, which is a mirror of `AbstractString`, just wrapping an `AbstractChar` instead.

To handle styling information, a new type is added to contain all possibly relevant styling information — `Face`. This is very much inspired by https://www.gnu.org/software/emacs/manual/html_node/elisp/Faces.html, as I have found this system to work rather well in practice (why invent a new approach when there's a lovely battle-tested one we can be inspired by? 😉).

The main way faces work is by having a global `Dics{Symbol, Face}` of face names. This has a number of advantages:
- It makes it easy to reference faces by their name
- Packages can easily add new faces
- It makes it possible for faces to inherit from other faces (e.g. have a `:julia_help_prompt` face that inherits from `:julia_prompt`)

Conversion functions to accept and load face specifications from a `Dict{String, Any}` are also added. By loading `~/.julia/config/faces.toml` this resolves #41435.

To handle printing faces well, we need to inquire about the terminal a fair bit more, and so I've replaced `tcap` with a terminfo parser implemented in accordance with `term(5)`.

Lastly, we have a string macro (`@S_str`) that was rather nasty to write, but is very convenient.

#### Currently unimplemented

- `replace`
- property removal

## Usage examples

The example from the `@S_str` docs

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235492934-d3299633-36fe-4a21-8321-83e24d8c4a6f.png)

Easy nested styling

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235493196-753f50e4-3acc-4821-9146-8c1aef5e6cd1.png)

No more issues with unterminated ANSI codes

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235494848-eccefbda-f943-4c1d-85a0-dbd2297b9217.png)

String functions operate on the unstyled content, while preserving styling where possible

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235496767-1d537db9-befc-4ee6-b482-e91778e32ceb.png)

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235496290-d3b6bb79-9c33-4dce-bf6f-b20d696cc5b2.png)

Styled text will actually automatically fall back to the un-styled form when printed to an `io` with `:color != true`.

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235496566-330a621d-e0d9-4b36-8714-e9d225e89ef7.png)

Incrementally styling a string, and changing face definitions on-the-fly (note: this uses currently "private API" functions)

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235499614-ce8d5aca-a561-4280-979e-d6c176efd1df.png)

## Fancier usage examples

The result of the example "banner using `S"..."`" commit

![image](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/assets/20903656/1e5cdf8a-4e3e-48ef-ae94-a0850c484270)

A fancier `^R` implementation, that's been implemented using `StyledString`s and `JuliaSyntax` that would be a massive pain to do via `printstyled` et al.

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235492830-4e0476d1-0716-48df-be8a-b1f26fe9b53d.png)

With very little work, docstring previews could look like this

![image](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/assets/20903656/f546de5c-e2ec-4ba8-bcf7-d2ff714bb928)

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48495","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48495/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48495/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48495/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48495","id":1567777141,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM5dcmF1","number":48495,"title":"Print `NamedTuple` types using macro syntax","user":{"login":"jkrumbiegel","id":22495855,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIyNDk1ODU1","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/22495855?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jkrumbiegel","html_url":"https://github.com/jkrumbiegel","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jkrumbiegel/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jkrumbiegel/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jkrumbiegel/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jkrumbiegel/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jkrumbiegel/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jkrumbiegel/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jkrumbiegel/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jkrumbiegel/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jkrumbiegel/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2023-02-02T10:33:23Z","updated_at":"2023-02-02T10:33:23Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"The `@NamedTuple` macro was added in v1.5 and makes it easier to specify `NamedTuple` types by pairing field names and field types.

However, in stack traces and such, `NamedTuple`s tend to make things hard to read because they are printed as they are stored, as a tuple of symbols plus a tuple of types. I suggest changing the print behavior of `NamedTuple`s such that they use the macro version. This would still make printed types pasteable in the REPL, because the macro is always available. But it would give a benefit in readability, which we sorely need wherever large types are printed.

Here's an example implementation with a stack trace before and after:

```julia
julia> nt = (; int = 1, double = 2.0, float = 3f0, nt = (first = 1, second = 2.0), string = "4", char = '5')
(int = 1, double = 2.0, float = 3.0f0, nt = (first = 1, second = 2.0), string = "4", char = '5')

julia> f(x) = error("")
f (generic function with 1 method)

julia> f(nt)
ERROR:
Stacktrace:
 [1] error(s::String)
   @ Base ./error.jl:35
 [2] f(x::NamedTuple{(:int, :double, :float, :nt, :string, :char), Tuple{Int64, Float64, Float32, NamedTuple{(:first, :second), Tuple{Int64, Float64}}, String, Char}})
   @ Main ./REPL[5]:1
 [3] top-level scope
   @ REPL[6]:1

julia> function Base.show(io::IO, T::Type{<:NamedTuple})
           print(io, "@NamedTuple{")
           for (i, (sym, type)) in enumerate(zip(fieldnames(T), fieldtypes(T)))
               i > 1 && print(io, ", ")
               print(io, sym, "::", type)
           end
           print(io, "}")
       end

julia> f(nt)
ERROR:
Stacktrace:
 [1] error(s::String)
   @ Base ./error.jl:35
 [2] f(x::@NamedTuple{int::Int64, double::Float64, float::Float32, nt::@NamedTuple{first::Int64, second::Float64}, string::String, char::Char})
   @ Main ./REPL[5]:1
 [3] top-level scope
   @ REPL[8]:1
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48495/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48495/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

I want to install a package but it gives me a mass of information which I find hard to process (sometimes it's much larger than this). I usually look through it for some packages I recognize and try randomly removing them to see if that helps. I usually have to guess a number of times. 

Is there a simple rule for what I should do?

It would be helpful if the resolver could suggest some commands using heuristics for what I can remove in order to get the package I want. Some heuristics might be: a package is not recently used, hasn't been upgraded lately, is behind the upstream version (I'm not sure which of these are possible/make sense). 


For example it might say:

```
Suggestions (any of these should allow installing AlgebraOfGraphics@0.6.11):

    rm GLMakie, Observables # least recently used

    rm AbstractPlotting # most behind

  1. `.ji` contains a full-header and all the source for Revise
  2. pkimage/`.so` That contains all the data and the native code
2. During compilation we delay resolution of functions already present in a pkgimage
    This uses `jl_code_instance_t` as the relocation key and emits a table into the binary code
    against which to later perform relocation.
